selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":89,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"hairdresser","city_of_living":"Los Angeles","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"no formal education","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"current_marital_status":"divorced","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Christianity","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["gardening","birdwatching","knitting","volunteering","rock climbing"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":57,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":88,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":67,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":29,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":72,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"Well, dear, I suppose I don't have much experience with all these 'social media' things, as I don't use them much myself. It feels like a whole different world, a whirlwind of noise that I just try to steer clear of, much like I try to keep the weeds down in my garden so they don't take over everything. If I were to try and think about this fake content business, it reminds me of gossip, doesn't it? The kind that gets spread around the salon, and you just have to decide if it’s true or if it’s just idle chatter designed to upset someone. Because I have my own quiet life—my gardening, the birds, my knitting—I tend to filter things through a lens of caution. I’ve always believed in looking at things closely, giving them the time they deserve before I let them settle in my head, much like how I examine a new pattern before I start knitting it. When things look too perfect, or when they stir up a terrible amount of anger or fear without any real substance, that’s when I get my intuition telling me to be very careful. It’s a kind of natural skepticism, I suppose, born from seeing enough of life’s difficult patches. I don't feel the urge to dive into the digital fray, but if one *had* to manage it, I'd advise treating it like a fragile piece of china; you don't just throw it around, you handle it with the utmost care, questioning the source every single time. For me, the best management strategy is simply distance, retreating to the tangible things—the feel of the soil, the pattern of the stitches—where what you see is usually what you get, unlike this swirling chaos online.","reasoning_summary":"The persona is 89, depressed, low agreeableness, and has no social media use, suggesting a highly cautious, skeptical, and somewhat withdrawn tone. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Well, dear, I suppose I don't have much experience with all these 'social media' things, as I don't use them much myself. It feels like a whole different world, a whirlwind of noise that I just try to steer clear of, much like I try to keep the weeds down in my garden so they don't take over everything. If I were to try and think about this fake content business, it reminds me of gossip, doesn't it? The kind that gets spread around the salon, and you just have to decide if it’s true or if it’s just idle chatter designed to upset someone. Because I have my own quiet life—my gardening, the birds, my knitting—I tend to filter things through a lens of caution. I’ve always believed in looking at things closely, giving them the time they deserve before I let them settle in my head, much like how I examine a new pattern before I start knitting it. When things look too perfect, or when they stir up a terrible amount of anger or fear without any real substance, that’s when I get my intuition telling me to be very careful. It’s a kind of natural skepticism, I suppose, born from seeing enough of life’s difficult patches. I don't feel the urge to dive into the digital fray, but if one *had* to manage it, I'd advise treating it like a fragile piece of china; you don't just throw it around, you handle it with the utmost care, questioning the source every single time. For me, the best management strategy is simply distance, retreating to the tangible things—the feel of the soil, the pattern of the stitches—where what you see is usually what you get, unlike this swirling chaos online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is 89, depressed, low agreeableness, and has no social media use, suggesting a highly cautious, skeptical, and somewhat withdrawn tone. The response must reflect a perspective based on real-world, analog experience (gardening, knitting, salon gossip) rather than digital expertise. The discussion must frame 'fake content' as an analog concept (gossip, untrustworthy information) and advocate for caution and distance, fitting the persona's low engagement with modern technology and high neuroticism/cautiousness.
response_parsed.tone: Cautious, world-weary, skeptical, and somewhat melancholic
